title: SACRAMENTO – Legislation to expand access to domestic violence services, authored by Assemblywoman Cottie Petrie-Norris, has passed the Senate Public Safety with unanimous bipartis

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "AB 689 will allow for survivors of domestic violence to seek help via text and other computer-based technologies.",
    "One in 3 women in America experience violence in the home.",
    "In California, 34.9% of women and 31.1% of men experience intimate partner physical violence, intimate partner sexual violence and/or intimate partner stalking in their lifetimes.",
    "The National Domestic Violence Hotline documented 29,659 contacts from California in 2019, the highest in the nation.",
    "The current requirement for domestic violence centers is limited in its definition to phone-based hotlines, preventing the expansion of other types of hotline services.",
    "Modernizing domestic violence shelter requirements to include the option of other technological platforms will allow centers to help more victims of domestic violence.",
    "The state will be able to track and collect more accurate data about domestic abuse by modernizing hotline services.",
    "Expanding the crisis line service to include texting or live chat gives survivors more options for accessing lifesaving services and support.",
    "A secure chat line platform has proven to be effective, particularly as it aligns with common modes of communication among youth and young adults.",
    "Chat and text-based hotlines are crucial for survivors of domestic violence who cannot safely call a hotline.",
    "Expanding access points to include chat and text-based options is key to breaking down barriers to survivor safety.",
    "Reports indicate that incidents of intimate partner violence increased in frequency and severity during COVID-19 lockdown orders."
  ]
}
```
